Title: King praises frontliners for dedication during Aidilfitri
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, paid a heartfelt tribute to the nation's security forces and enforcement agencies, praising their unwavering commitment and selfless sacrifices during the upcoming Aidilfitri festive season.
In a message shared on His Majesty's official Facebook page today, His Majesty expressed his deepest admiration for those who remain on the frontlines while the rest of the nation celebrates with their loved ones.
Sultan Ibrahim highlighted the dedication shown by these personnel, noting that they carry out their duties with a strong sense of responsibility to ensure public peace, order, and the well-being of the rakyat remain uncompromised.
"His Majesty highly values the sacrifices made by members of the security and enforcement agencies who remain on duty to ensure that peace, public order, and the prosperity of the people are always preserved, even as others celebrate Aidilfitri with their families," the post read.
Beyond words of praise, the King also offered a personal prayer for the men and women in uniform, asking for their continued protection, good health, and that their tasks be eased by the Almighty as they serve the country.
Earlier today, Sultan Ibrahim granted an audience to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim at Istana Bukit Tunku to discuss national matters.
In a fatherly reminder to the nation, His Majesty also turned his attention to those preparing for the balik kampung exodus, advising the public to exercise extreme caution on the roads to ensure they reach their destinations safely.
Sultan Ibrahim urged all road users to strictly abide by traffic laws and avoid rushing, emphasising that safety must be the priority to prevent any untoward incidents during the festive season.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family escapes unharmed after car catches fire en route for Aidilfitri
Article:
KUALA KRAI: A family of five travelling from Nilai to their hometown in Kok Lanas for Aidilfitri had a narrow escape when their car caught fire in Kampung Pahi, near Hospital Sultan Ismail Petra, last night.
Kuala Krai Fire and Rescue Station chief Senior Fire Officer Nik Ahmad Afsham Nik Pa said the station received an emergency call at 10.48pm.
Six personnel were deployed to the scene, where they found the Proton Waja already fully engulfed in flames.
"The driver noticed smoke coming from the front bonnet and immediately pulled over by the roadside. The family quickly removed their belongings, including bags of clothing," he told Berita Harian.
He said all five occupants managed to escape unhurt.
The operation was concluded at 11.33pm after the area was declared safe.
The cause of the fire is under investigation by forensic authorities.
Nik Ahmad Afsham added that the Kuala Krai Assistance Squad helped transport the family safely to Kok Lanas.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four fined RM750 each over drunken brawl
Article:
BATU PAHAT: Four men were fined RM750 each by the Magistrate's Court for their involvement in a drunken brawl at an entertainment outlet last week.
Nai Siang, 39, See Chee Rong, 29, Ong Poh Heng, 31, and Ter Aik Siong, 32, pleaded guilty before Magistrate Arun Noval Dass.
They were ordered to serve a month's jail if they failed to pay the fine. All four paid the fine.
According to the charge sheet, the men were accused of fighting and struggling with one another at a premises in Taman Flora at 12.15am on March 16.
The charge was framed under Section 160 of the Penal Code for fighting in a public place, which carries a maximum penalty of six months' imprisonment, a RM1,000 fine, or both, upon conviction.
The court heard that the men were drinking before an argument broke out. The dispute escalated into a fight, leading to them being kicked out of the premises.
The brawl continued in the car park before it was eventually broken up. All four sustained injuries to their bodies, hands, and foreheads, and received treatment at a hospital. They were subsequently arrested.
Deputy public prosecutor Firdaus Ruslan appeared for the prosecution, while the accused were represented by lawyer Tan Song Yan from the National Legal Aid Foundation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's inflation modestly up 1.4pct in Feb 2026: Statistics Department
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's inflation rose modestly to 1.4 per cent in February 2026, with the consumer price index (CPI) climbing to 136.0 from 134.1 a year earlier, the Statistics Department said.
In a statement today, chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said eight categories recorded slower year-on-year growth compared with January 2026.
These included insurance and financial services at 4.7 per cent (January 2026: 5.5 per cent), education 2.8 per cent (3.2 per cent), restaurant and accommodation services 2.5 per cent (3.0 per cent), and food and beverages 1.3 per cent (1.5 per cent).
Other groups showed marginal increases, with health rising 1.2 per cent (1.4 per cent), housing, water, electricity, gas, and other fuels up 1.1 per cent (1.2 per cent), and information and communication advancing 0.5 per cent (0.7 per cent).
Meanwhile, personal care, social protection, and miscellaneous goods and services increased to 6.9 per cent (6.6 per cent), while alcoholic beverages and tobacco rose to 2.6 per cent (2.5 per cent).
According to the department, about 59.7 per cent of items (342 of 573) recorded price increases, of which 333 items (97.4 per cent) increased by 10 per cent or less, while only nine items rose by more than 10 per cent in February 2026.
The remaining 190 items (33.2 per cent) declined, and 41 items remained unchanged.
The food and beverages group, which contributes 29.8 per cent of total CPI weight, rose slower at 1.3 per cent in February compared with 1.5 per cent in January, driven by food away from home (2.4 per cent; January: 2.5 per cent) and food at home (0.3 per cent; January: 0.6 per cent).
Inflation in the housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels group slowed to 1.1 per cent from 1.2 per cent in January, driven by water supply and miscellaneous services related to dwelling, 6.8 per cent (7.1 per cent), and actual rentals for housing, 1.5 per cent (1.9 per cent).
The department said the average price of unleaded petrol RON97 remained at RM3.11 per litre in February 2026.
The average price for diesel in Peninsular Malaysia rose to RM2.98 per litre from RM2.89 in January, while in Sabah, Sarawak and Labuan it remained at RM2.15 per litre. The average market price of RON95 increased slightly to RM2.55 per litre (January: RM2.54) compared with the subsidised price of RM1.99.
Mohd Uzir said eight states recorded inflation above the national rate of 1.4 per cent: Pahang (2.1 per cent), Federal Territory (FT) Labuan (2.1 per cent), Negeri Sembilan (2.0 per cent), Sabah (1.8 per cent), Johor (1.7 per cent), Pulau Pinang (1.6 per cent), Terengganu (1.6 per cent), and FT Kuala Lumpur (1.6 per cent). Kelantan recorded the lowest inflation at 0.2 per cent.
All states registered higher food and beverage inflation except Kelantan (-0.7 per cent). Seven states exceeded the national food and beverages inflation rate of 1.3 per cent, led by Negeri Sembilan (3.0 per cent), Pahang (2.3 per cent), Johor (2.1 per cent), FT Kuala Lumpur (1.8 per cent), Pulau Pinang (1.7 per cent), Kedah (1.6 per cent) and Melaka (1.4 per cent).
Month-on-month, headline inflation rose 0.2 per cent in February (January: 0.1 per cent), driven by housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels (0.7 per cent; January: 0.3 per cent), transport (0.2 per cent; -0.4 per cent) and clothing and footwear (0.1 per cent; 0.0 per cent). Five groups recorded no monthly changes: food and beverages, furnishings and household equipment, health, recreation, sport and culture, and insurance and financial services.
Regionally, Malaysia's 1.4 per cent inflation in February 2026 was lower than Indonesia (4.8 per cent), Vietnam (3.4 per cent), the Philippines (2.4 per cent) and South Korea (2.0 per cent), but higher than China (1.3 per cent) and Thailand (-0.9 per cent).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lessons in asymmetrical warfare concept in modern conflicts
Article:
LETTER:
THOMAS Edward Lawrence, famously known as Lawrence of Arabia, left an enduring legacy through his mastery of guerrilla tactics during the First World War.
His philosophy of using a smaller force to neutralise a superior adversary through unconventional means is being mirrored with striking precision in contemporary conflicts, such as the Ukraine war and the Iran conflict.
The current landscape demonstrates that the military fraternity requires a dedicated pool of manpower equipped with innovative ideas.
Success in modern combat is no longer about traditional might; it demands individuals who can think beyond conventional boundaries and possess a "cunning mindset" that effectively "jives" with emerging technology.
Next, innovation in warfare must transcend the narrow confines of traditional military personnel or industries, specifically those specialising in artificial intelligence (AI), cyber-security, and drone technology.
This reflects a shift where asymmetrical warfare is no longer limited to physical disruption but is increasingly focused on the dismantling of digital infrastructures.
It is worth noting that, while asymmetrical tactics may not be as "decisive" in ending a war, their psychological impact is profound.
These methods allow a state that may not be superior in its military arsenal to give a "bloody nose" to a much stronger adversary.
Asymmetrical warfare often evolves into gruelling wars of attrition.
This was evident in the Ukrainian conflict, where initial estimates suggested Kyiv would fall within weeks; instead, the struggle has endured.
Asymmetrical warfare should be integrated into formal military doctrines, particularly for states operating with smaller budgets, to ensure a nation remains ready for the shifting and unconventional demands of modern combat.
AHMAD TAJUDDIN MOHD SAID
Malaysian Institute of Defence and Security (MiDAS)
Kuala Lumpur
*The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Non-national carmakers face pressure amid rising competition
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: National carmakers are poised to sustain sales momentum throughout the year, while non-national players may continue to face pressure, according to Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB).
This comes as competition intensifies with new entrants offering more competitive pricing, updated designs and enhanced product features.
"Order backlogs remain strong amid robust demand for national marques. We expect sector earnings to remain under pressure in 2026, due to lower sales volumes and rising operating costs from intensified promotional activities," it said.
HLIB said the impact would be partly mitigated by a stronger ringgit against the US dollar and Japanese yen.
The firm named MBM Resources Bhd and Sime Darby Bhd as its top picks in the automotive sector.
"MBM benefits from its strong exposure to Perodua, while Sime is supported by sustained strength in its Industrial segment, particularly in Australia, a recovery in its China Motors division, and stronger contributions from UMW across all segments.
"Both MBM and Sime also offer attractive dividend yields of five to seven per cent," it added.
Malaysia's total industry volume (TIV) fell to 52,400 units in February 2026, mainly due to fewer working days during the extended Chinese New Year holidays.
Year-to-date, TIV edged up 0.7 per cent year-on-year to 116,700 units.
HLIB said the growth was driven by strong Proton sales, partially offset by weaker performance from Perodua and Japanese original equipment manufacturers.
Electric vehicle sales totalled 9,900 units, accounting for 8.5 per cent of TIV, led largely by the recently launched Proton eMas 5.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bahulu gets modern makeover to win over younger generation
Article:
SEREMBAN: A traditional kuih is getting a contemporary update, as one entrepreneur reinvents bahulu with modern flavours to appeal to a younger generation.
Bahulu Sendeng founder Siti Suhana Mohd Daralsalam, 42, has introduced 13 variations of the classic treat, including Nutella, chocolate, pandan, strawberry and red velvet.
She said the move was aimed at preserving the identity of the traditional kuih while attracting new customers, particularly children and teenagers.
Boxes of 20 pieces are priced between RM12 and RM15 for the flavoured versions, while the original is sold at RM10.
Siti Suhana said the idea came from a desire to expand her customer base.
"Initially, I only sold one flavour and most buyers were older customers. I felt there should be something different, so I started with pandan," she said.
"The response was encouraging, and I gradually introduced more varieties until we now have 13 flavours."
The Seremban native runs the business with her husband, Mohd Shahrizat Rahim, 46. They began in 2017 selling popcorn before shifting to bahulu two years later.
"At that time, the popcorn business was doing well, but I felt the product might only last about five years. So I started preparing to introduce bahulu as a new offering," she said.
The name "Sendeng" is derived from the shop's location near Masjid Jamek Dato' Kelana Petra Sendeng.
From two ovens at the start, the business now operates eight to meet growing demand, particularly on weekends.
Sales can reach about 300 boxes a day, generating up to RM3,000, while monthly revenue averages around RM40,000 and can rise to RM80,000 during festive seasons. Nutella and red velvet are among the bestsellers.
Siti Suhana said the bahulu's appeal lies in its soft texture and freshness, with each batch baked in about 15 minutes and sold immediately rather than stored.
The products are also available at selected supermarkets and promoted through programmes such as the Malaysia Agriculture, Horticulture and Agrotourism (MAHA) Exhibition.
Despite rising raw material costs, including eggs, and increased competition, she said efforts were made to keep prices affordable.
Looking ahead, she hopes to establish a one-stop bahulu centre as a food destination in Negeri Sembilan while continuing to innovate traditional kuih.
Customer Lydia Hendroff, 51, said the wide variety of flavours attracted her to try the product.
"This is my first time buying them because I'm curious about the many flavour choices. I've never seen bahulu with this many varieties, and I want to share them with my friends," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IGP: Police treat Zamri Vinoth, Arun Dorasamy equally
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have denied treating Zamri Vinoth and Arun Dorasamy differently, emphasising that action was taken strictly in accordance with the law.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said that although all four individuals were arrested under Section 505(b) of the Penal Code, one of the cases also involved an offence under the Sedition Act 1948.
"Offences differ; some are bailable, some are not. These actions are based on the legal provisions," he told reporters at Bukit Aman today.
He added "The public may perceive that they are being treated differently, but we are enforcing the law according to its provisions."
MORE TO COME...
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two charged with neighbour's murder
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: Two men were charged in the Magistrate's Court here today with the murder of their neighbour on March 10.
S. Dashratmen, 18, and P. Velson Paul, 21, nodded to indicate they understood the charge after it was read out before Magistrate Mohamad Syaiful Akmal Mohammad Razi.
No plea was recorded as murder cases fall under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
The duo are accused of causing the death of Kavin Raj, 27, at a house in Taman Kempas Fasa 2 between 12.30am and 1.30am on March 10.
The charge, under Section 302 of the Penal Code, carries the death penalty or a prison sentence of up to 40 years and at least 12 strokes of the cane upon conviction.
Deputy Public Prosecutor R. Lushani appeared for the prosecution, while the accused were represented by counsel RSN Rayer.
Rayer requested the court to allow his clients to receive medical treatment for foot injuries allegedly sustained while in custody.
However, the court ruled that the accused could receive treatment at the prison where they are being held.
The court set June 15 for mention pending forensic, chemical, and autopsy reports before the case is transferred to the High Court.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AstraZeneca introduces rare disease drug Soliris in Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: AstraZeneca has made its rare disease treatment, Soliris, available in Malaysia, expanding options for patients with complex neurological autoimmune conditions and severe blood disorders.
In a statement, the biopharmaceutical company said the therapy has shown improved clinical outcomes in treating conditions such as generalised myasthenia gravis (gMG), neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome (aHUS) and paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH).
Soliris works by targeting the complement system — part of the immune response — by blocking the C5 protein to help control excessive immune activity linked to these diseases.
AstraZeneca Malaysia country president Svetlana Yanchuk said the treatment's availability reflects the company's commitment to supporting patients living with rare diseases.
"With Soliris, we can advance our efforts in Malaysia to transform the lives of people living with rare diseases and help them live more meaningful lives," she said.
She added that continued research and development were crucial in delivering innovative treatments, noting that AstraZeneca's acquisition of Alexion in 2021 had strengthened its capabilities in developing therapies targeting the immune system's complement pathway.
"Moving forward, we remain committed to advancing our leadership in this area and advocating for the rare disease community, including introducing next-generation treatments," she said.
The company noted that rare diseases are often difficult to diagnose, with patients frequently facing delays, repeated testing and misdiagnosis.
Globally, about 300 million people live with rare diseases, many of which are serious and progressive. In Malaysia, 491 rare diseases had been recorded as of 2020, although many cases remain underdiagnosed.
Consultant haematologist at Hospital Umum Sarawak Dr Chew Lee Ping said aHUS and PNH are linked to immune system abnormalities affecting the blood.
"aHUS can cause kidney damage, while PNH is a chronic and potentially life-threatening disorder that leads to the premature breakdown of red blood cells and increases the risk of blood clots," she said.
Consultant neurologist at Sunway Medical Centre Dr Hiew Fu Liong said gMG and NMOSD affect the nervous system and can lead to serious complications.
"gMG is a chronic autoimmune neuromuscular disease that causes muscle weakness, while NMOSD affects the optic nerves and spinal cord and can result in vision loss, paralysis and other neurological complications," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia trade surplus jumps 137.6pct to RM38.7bil in Jan-Feb
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's trade surplus widened 137.6 per cent to RM38.67 billion in the first two months of this year, as total trade rose 11 per cent to RM516.89 billion, Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (Matrade) said.
Export growth was driven by the electrical and electronics sector, which expanded 34.2 per cent and accounted for nearly half of total exports at RM131.34 billion.
Machinery, equipment and parts rose 39.8 per cent to RM12.49 billion, while palm oil and agricultural products increased 6.6 per cent to RM12.41 billion.
Traditional export destinations such as the United States and Hong Kong continued to record strong growth of 38.1 per cent and 45.8 per cent, respectively, even as Malaysia pivots towards high-growth emerging markets.
These include Uzbekistan, Mexico, Brazil and South Africa, all of which posted double-digit growth, while exports to Sudan and Ethiopia grew more than sevenfold and eightfold, respectively.
This was driven by Malaysia's diversification strategy to mitigate risks associated with traditional markets and global volatility.
The ongoing conflict in West Asia, which has disrupted global trade routes and shipping lanes, has pushed up logistics costs, with strained supply chains remaining a key concern among exporters.
Matrde chief executive officer Abu Bakar Yusof said nearly two-thirds of Malaysian exporters expect to be affected, citing shipment delays and rising freight costs.
He described the situation as a "double-ended blockade" affecting cargo flows.
"Our immediate priority is to mitigate the 'double-ended' blockade affecting cargo flows by leveraging our presence in West Asia to obtain real-time feedback on logistical challenges that may impact Malaysia's exports," he said in a statement.
Matrade is activating its network of offices across key Middle Eastern cities, including Dubai, Jeddah, Cairo and Doha, to gather real-time intelligence and support exporters navigating the disruptions.
Businesses are advised to explore alternative logistics strategies, such as rerouting shipments through safer ports like Fujairah or Salalah, as well as expanding into less-affected regions such as South Asia, Latin America and Africa.
"We are sharing critical ground intelligence with the business community and advising on alternative logistics routes and supply chain adjustments to bypass disruptions in the Red Sea.
"For this purpose, Matrade has also been engaging with exporters through trade and industry associations such as the Federation of Malaysian Manufacturers, the Malaysian Consortium of Mid-Tier Companies and the Malaysian International Chamber of Commerce and Industry," he added.
Looking ahead, Abu Bakar said Malaysia must sustain its current growth momentum while navigating an increasingly challenging global trade environment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chegubard expelled from Bersatu, says party no longer serves rakyat
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bersatu Port Dickson chief Badrul Hisham Shaharin, better known as Chegubard, has been expelled from the party.
He said the party's disciplinary board secretary informed him via WhatsApp at 6pm yesterday.
Chegubard claimed Bersatu no longer champions the people's cause and instead serves certain groups.
"As expected, I was expelled at 6pm yesterday through WhatsApp," he said, adding he will issue an official statement tonight.
Elected Bersatu Port Dickson chief in Nov 2024, he said he will continue fighting for the people and combating corruption.
He was previously a Parti Keadilan Rakyat (PKR) member before being sacked in 2016, and joined Bersatu in 2020.
On March 10, he said he received a show-cause letter from the party's disciplinary board over allegations, including tarnishing the party's image.
He was given until Tuesday to respond but refused, describing the allegations as unreasonable.
He also claimed the party is tightly controlled, with members not free to voice their views.
Bersatu has been facing internal turmoil involving its president Tan Sri  Muhyiddin Yassin and former deputy president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin, leading to the expulsion of the Larut MP, who is also the Opposition leader, on Feb 13.
Several other leaders were also sacked or suspended, including former vice-president Datuk Seri Dr Ronald Kiandee and former Women's chief Datuk Mas Ermieyati Samsudin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IGP warns against racial provocation, urges restraint
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have urged the public to refrain from making provocative statements on race and religion, warning that such remarks could threaten national stability.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said recent developments showed how administrative issues could quickly escalate into racial and religious tensions if not handled carefully.
"Malaysia is a multiracial and multireligious country. Sensitivities must be respected. I urge all parties to reduce negative comments that touch on religion and race," he told reporters at Bukit Aman on Thursday.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Victor Chin abroad amid money laundering probe, says IGP
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are continuing their investigation into businessman Victor Chin under anti-money laundering laws, with the suspect believed to be abroad.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said the probe was being conducted under the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act (Amla).
"Victor Chin is not in the country at the moment. Efforts are ongoing to obtain cooperation from relevant parties," he told reporters at Bukit Aman today.
He said police may seek the assistance of their foreign counterparts.
"Authorities are working with multiple agencies to advance the case," he said.
Raids
on several premises linked to Chin over his alleged involvement in a purported "corporate mafia" led to the seizure of luxury watches, jewellery, cash in various currencies, and two cars.
Chin has
denied
any involvement in the matter.
Previously, Bukit Aman Anti-Money Laundering Crime Investigation Unit chief Datuk Fazlisyam Abdul Majid said the raids also resulted in the seizure of several documents relevant to the probe.
The case is being investigated under Section 4(1) of Amla, which criminalises money laundering by anyone engaging, directly or indirectly, in transactions, transfers, or the concealment of proceeds from unlawful activities.
Those convicted face up to 15 years in prison and a fine of at least RM5 million or five times the value of the proceeds, whichever is higher.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 3 million vehicles expected on KLK, LPT1 during Aidilfitri
Article:
KUANTAN: A total of 3.03 million vehicles are expected to traverse the Kuala Lumpur–Karak (KLK) Expressway and the East Coast Expressway Phase 1 (LPT1) during the upcoming Aidilfitri festive period.
Pahang Public Works, Transport, and Health Committee chairman Datuk Razali Kassim said the surge in traffic is projected to occur over a 12-day period between March 18 and March 29.
Providing a breakdown, he said that 2.1 million vehicles were expected to use the KLK, while 934,000 were projected for the LPT1.
"The average daily peak traffic was estimated at 78,000 vehicles. However, we anticipated the highest traffic volume to occur on March 28, with approximately 106,000 vehicles on the road," he said.
"Given this significant increase in traffic flow, I urge all road users to remain vigilant, strictly adhere to traffic regulations, and prioritise safety to ensure every journey is smooth and secure."
Razali made the remarks during the Road Safety Advocacy Program held in conjunction with Aidilfitri 2026 at the Gambang Rest and Service (R&R) area (eastbound) of the LPT1 here last night.
Also in attendance was Pahang Road Transport Department (RTD) director Syed Ahmad Khirulanwar Alyahya Syed Abdul Rahman.
Addressing safety concerns, Razali revealed that the police had identified 38 accident-prone hotspots along the routes. Additionally, 22 "hot zones" with high potential for traffic congestion across Pahang had been identified.
"The state government remains committed to supporting all initiatives by the RTD and relevant agencies to ensure the safety of road users. This includes strategic collaborations in enforcement, road safety education, and public awareness campaigns. Such cooperation is vital to strengthening legal compliance and safety awareness across the state," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Police continue search for activist Tamim believed to be abroad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are tracking activist Tamim Dahri Abdul Razak, believed to have fled the country, with intelligence indicating he may have travelled to Thailand or the Middle East.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said authorities were still working to locate the individual.
"Our reports indicate that he left for Thailand, and there is also information suggesting he may be in the Holy Land," he told reporters at Bukit Aman today.
He said that charges would be filed in court and that action would be taken once the suspect was located.
"Investigations are ongoing as police attempt to obtain further leads on his
whereabouts
," he added.
It was reported that the case against Tamim, who was due to be charged in Langkawi on March 17 for allegedly desecrating a Hindu religious symbol, had been postponed until May 17.
Magistrate Hemy Annerina Haja Mydin set the new date after the prosecution applied for a postponement, as Tamim was currently overseas.
State prosecution Director Datuk Khairul Anuar Abd Halim said the authorities needed about two months to enable the activist to be brought to court.
Checks with Thai immigration officials revealed that Tamim had flown to Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, on March 15.
A total of 192 police reports had been lodged against Tamim.
He was alleged to have desecrated a soolam, a holy symbol of Hinduism, at the site of an old temple in the area.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lorry driver charged over Bangsar armed gang robbery
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A lorry driver pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court today to a charge of armed gang robbery at an Ayurvedic clinic in Bangsar.
Sangara Lingam Packiry, 50, entered the plea after the charge was read out before judge Suhaila Haron.
According to the charge sheet, Sangara and another person still at large, armed with a parang, robbed a 35-year-old Pakistani clinic worker, Sajid Ahmed Abbasi, of a ring.
The offence was allegedly committed on the afternoon of March 4, 2025.
The charge was framed under Section 395 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 397 of the same code, which carries a maximum penalty of 20 years' imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Nor Aisyah Mohamed Zanyuin requested that no bail be offered as the case involved the use of a dangerous weapon.
"However, should the court exercise its discretion to grant bail, the prosecution requests a sum of RM10,000 with additional conditions, namely reporting to the nearest police station and not harassing prosecution witnesses," she said.
Defence counsel Tripatjit Singh requested a lower bail amount on humanitarian grounds and his client's family background.
"My client works as a lorry driver and has two children who are still studying. He is the sole breadwinner and cares for his mother, who suffers from kidney disease.
"The accused also has health issues related to his intestines. We have no objection to the additional conditions but request the bail amount be reduced," he said.
Suhaila allowed bail at RM7,000 with one surety and ordered the accused to report to a police station monthly.
"The accused is prohibited from harassing the victim and prosecution witnesses until the case is concluded," she said.
The court fixed April 17 for mention and the submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Indira Gandhi case: IGP vows continued probe after committal bid
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have pledged to continue investigating the long-standing Indira Gandhi case following a committal application filed against the office of the Inspector-General of Police.
Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said he had met with Indira Gandhi, her family and legal representatives to discuss the matter.
"This case has been ongoing for a long time. I have met with her and her lawyers, and we will conduct investigations based on the information provided," he told reporters at Bukit Aman today.
He said that police remain committed to assisting in resolving the case, while acknowledging that legal action taken by Indira is her right.
"We will do our best within our responsibilities," he added.
Police said there were no new developments, but
investigations
would proceed based on any new information received.
Indira filed an application for committal action against the office of the Inspector-General of Police for contempt of court, accusing police of failing to arrest her ex-husband and locate her abducted daughter for over a decade.
The move stems from a 2014 Ipoh High Court recovery order which compelled police to find Indira's daughter, Prasana Diksa, and arrest her ex-husband, Muhammad Riduan Abdullah.
Indira's lawyer, Rajesh Nagarajan, told the New Straits Times that despite many years, the court order had yet to be executed.
"Indira is disappointed with the lackadaisical attitude of the police in carrying out their duties," he said.
"This action is being taken because the police failed to discharge their duties under the Police Act, which is why they are in contempt of court."
The committal proceedings, filed under Order 52 of the Rules of Court 2012 in December 2025, were slated for hearing at the Ipoh High Court on April 24.
The judge will decide whether to grant Indira leave for a full hearing.
Meanwhile, time is running out for Indira, whose legal battle to reunite with Prasana will end early next month when the girl turns 18.
She will then lose recourse under the Child Act 2001 to compel authorities to return her daughter, who was just 11 months old when abducted by her ex-husband.
Indira's long ordeal began in 2009 with a legal challenge against Riduan's unilateral conversion of their three children to Islam without her consent. In 2010, she was granted custody of all three, but Riduan refused to comply and fled with Prasana.
The Ipoh High Court quashed the conversions in 2013, only for the Court of Appeal to reverse the ruling in 2015. The Federal Court subsequently nullified the unilateral conversions of the three children in January 2018.
The case has drawn national attention to issues of child custody, religious conversion and police accountability in interfaith disputes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Police seek international cooperation in Bloomberg probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are seeking international cooperation in a probe involving Bloomberg, with some individuals linked to the case believed to be based overseas.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said officers had visited Bloomberg's office at Menara Petronas and interviewed several staff members.
"We were informed that some individuals connected to the matter are based abroad.
"Further action will require cooperation through government-to-government channels or relevant agencies," he told reporters at Bukit Aman today.
He added that cross-border coordination would be crucial in advancing the investigation.
It was reported that the government is leaving it to the police to investigate allegations of a plot to topple the administration.
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil described the matter as serious and said it should be handled by the relevant authorities.
Earlier, Khalid said authorities were probing an alleged plan to destabilise the government and undermine national stability.
He added that the allegations involved a local influential figure and an international media agency, without naming either party.
Meanwhile, news portal MalaysiaNow
reported
that police had visited Bloomberg's Kuala Lumpur office to identify the journalist behind investigative reports on Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki's shareholdings.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police widen probe into alleged plot to topple government
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have expanded their investigation into an alleged plot to topple the government and undermine national stability, recording statements from 14 individuals so far.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said investigators are continuing efforts to identify and call up more people believed to be linked to the case.
"We have taken statements from 14 individuals so far, and there are about 14 more we may call," he told reporters at Bukit Aman on Thursday.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dodol gula kabung keeps tradition alive in Raub
Article:
RAUB: Ulu Dong, a place once known nationwide for a brutal political murder ritual in the early 1990s, now offers a peaceful environment across its several villages.
Along the entrance road to Ulu Dong, a small workshop stood beside a house in Kampung Padang Peri, where a robust man in his seventies was still peeling the husks of mature coconuts by hand.
Mohd Isa Ishak, 74, a retired government officer, remained active by producing dodol gula kabung (palm sugar) entirely by hand.
According to him, making dodol manually required considerable physical effort and served as excellent cardio exercise.
While most dodol producers had switched to gas stoves and specialised machines to stir the mixture, Mohd Isa chose to preserve the traditional method using a wood-fired stove and a wooden spatula.
This method produced dodol gula kabung with a distinctive taste and aroma reminiscent of the old days, and a coarse texture that set it apart from dodol made with granulated sugar.
Every Aidilfitri, Mohd Isa limited orders for his dodol gula kabung to maintain the quality of production. The sweet treat was sold at prices starting from RM32 for every 500g.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Top Glove faces margin pressure amid rising costs, stronger ringgit: HLIB Research
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Top Glove Corp Bhd is expected to face a challenging third quarter of the financial year ending Aug 31, 2026 (Q3 FY26) amid margin pressure from rising raw material costs and a stronger ringgit, Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB Research) said.
HLIB Research trimmed its FY26–28 forecasts by 6.9 per cent, 2.0 per cent and 1.1 per cent, respectively, citing potential flattish sales volumes and rising input costs.
Despite these near-term challenges, the firm kept a "Hold" rating on the stock with a revised target price of 54 sen, down from 60 sen, noting that recent share price corrections have largely priced in the risks.
It noted that the company's management plans to raise average selling prices (ASPs) more frequently, from monthly to weekly, to pass through higher nitrile butadiene rubber costs starting from the fourth week of March, with a more significant increase expected in April.
HLIB Research, however, cautions that these price adjustments may not fully offset the ringgit's 7.5 per cent appreciation against the US dollar since late October 2025 and ongoing cost pressures.
Additional uncertainties include logistical disruptions in the Middle East, which accounts for around 4.0 per cent of the group's sales volume, amid the Iran conflict.
"Given potential flattish volume, amid rising input costs of operations and ringgit strength, we expect Top Glove to register weaker quarter-on-quarter results in Q3," it said.
Echoing the same sentiment, CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd expects the company's core net profit to weaken as the ASP hikes only take effect in April and the typical 40–50 day inventory turnover period means higher input price increases will start impacting margins in April/May.
"Additionally, the absence of one-off tender sales will weigh on earnings, though this will be partially offset by the eventual rise in ASPs," it added.
CIMB Securities maintained a "Hold" rating with a lower target price of 63 sen from 68 sen previously.
However, the firm expects slightly higher earnings for FY26–28, lifting estimates by 4.0 per cent, 2.0 per cent and 1.0 per cent to reflect higher ASPs, partially offset by raw material costs and a weaker US dollar against the ringgit.
It also noted that the absence of one-off tender sales in Q3 could weigh on earnings, although price adjustments scheduled for April may mitigate the impact in subsequent months.
CIMB Securities said Top Glove has begun issuing price increase notices to customers to manage escalating input costs driven by geopolitical tensions and a weaker US dollar.
It said ASPs are expected to rise by US$7–RM9 per 1,000 pieces depending on the product type, though price-sensitive customers may temporarily switch to natural latex gloves, which are US$1–2 cheaper.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yen under pressure as BOJ and Fed hold rates steady
Article:
SINGAPORE: The yen teetered on the brink of its weakest level in two years in Asian morning trading on Thursday, struggling to find footing against a resurgent dollar as the Bank of Japan held rates steady in the shadow of the Iran conflict.
The yen was 0.1 per cent firmer at 159.655 per dollar, off its weakest levels in two years, as the BOJ kept its rate at 0.75 per cent and maintained its assessment that the economy was recovering moderately. Attention turns next to a press conference in a few hours where BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda will explain the central bank's decision.
"The Bank of Japan gave little away when it decided to keep interest rates unchanged today, but we still think it will hike rates again at its next meeting in April," analysts from Capital Economics wrote in a report.
"The short statement provided little insight into the future course of policy. We'll therefore have to wait for Governor Ueda's press conference later today for more insights into the bank's thinking."
Earlier in the day, Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said authorities are on heightened alert for currency market volatility and recent movements have been driven partly by speculators.
The Bank of Japan's decision comes midway through a pivotal stretch of major central bank meetings, as traders hunt for clues on how policymakers will respond to the energy price shock.
The Federal Open Market Committee held interest rates steady on Wednesday and projected higher inflation, steady unemployment and a single reduction in borrowing costs this year, a path that Fed Chair Jerome Powell said was subject to unusually high uncertainty as policymakers take stock of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran.
"Chair Powell was extremely vague on how the FOMC would respond to the war, repeatedly refusing to make conjectures on whether inflation or employment effects would dominate," said Steve Englander, global head of G10 FX research at Standard Chartered in New York.
"The hawkish part was the frustration Powell expressed at the slow pace of disinflation, very explicitly conditioning further policy rate cuts on inflation moving closer to target."
The dollar eased off a two-day high on Thursday as traders assessed the Fed's decision to hold rates against the backdrop of accelerating US inflation amid a raging Middle East conflict and surging oil prices.
The US dollar index, which measures the greenback's strength against a basket of six currencies, edged down 0.1 per cent to 100.07, near its highest levels over the last four months, as traders reined in bets that the Fed would cut interest rates later this year.
The Fed's decision came after data released on Wednesday showed the biggest increase in producer prices for seven months in February, driven by higher costs for services and a range of goods before the start of the war in the Middle East.
Financial markets are almost fully pricing in a hold at the US central bank's April 29 meeting, with expectations of further easing pushed out to 2027. Fed funds futures imply that the odds of a rate cut in December are little better than a coin toss, according to the CME Group's FedWatch tool.
Oil prices climbed further, with Brent crude futures rising 3.8 per cent to US$111.42 a barrel after Iran attacked several energy facilities across the Middle East following a strike on its South Pars gas field.
The euro climbed 0.3 per cent to US$1.1482, while the British pound edged up 0.2 per cent to US$1.3287. Both the European Central Bank and the Bank of England are expected to keep rates on hold when they announce policy decisions later on Thursday.
The Australian dollar edged up 0.4 per cent to US$0.7053, after data for February showed unemployment ticked up to 4.3 per cent, slightly above market estimates, and the Reserve Bank of Australia on Thursday warned the conflict in the Middle East was a material risk to the domestic economy.
The New Zealand dollar was up 0.4 per cent at US$0.5821 after official data released earlier showed gross domestic product rose 0.2 per cent in the fourth quarter on the prior quarter, but was weaker than analysts' expectations and the central bank's forecast.
"While less than forecast, the figures support the story that the economy was regaining some momentum before the latest oil shock hit," analysts from Westpac wrote in a research note.
The Reserve Bank of New Zealand also said it intended to make changes to its approach to Open Market Operations.
Against the Chinese yuan, the US dollar was down 0.1 per cent at 6.8978 yuan in offshore trade.
Bitcoin was flat at US$71,224.36, while ether was up 0.9 per cent at US$2,207.96.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hari Raya Aidilfitri @ Istana Arau celebration falls on first day of Syawal
Article:
KANGAR: The Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebration at Istana Arau will be held from 9.30am to noon on the first day of Syawal, following the Aidilfitri prayer.
The Office of the Raja of Perlis, Syed Sirajuddin Jamalullail, said in a statement today that attendees are encouraged to wear national attire and dress modestly while on the palace grounds.
"Wearing shorts and immodest clothing is not permitted," the statement read.
It said Tuanku Syed Sirajuddin, the Raja Muda of Perlis, Tuanku Syed Faizuddin Putra Jamalullail, and the Raja Puan Muda of Perlis, Tuanku Dr Hajah Lailatul Shahreen Akashah Khalil, will also perform the Aidilfitri prayer, scheduled to begin at 8 am, at Dataran Istiadat Istana Arau.
Attendees are advised to bring their own prayer mats, arrive in a state of ablution or perform ablution beforehand at the Arau State Mosque, and carry shoe bags for convenience.
In the event of unfavourable weather, the prayer will be moved to the mosque's prayer hall.
The office also said that the road in front of Istana Arau will be temporarily closed, with parking available around the Arau State Mosque and the Civic Hall under the supervision of traffic police.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Retrenched factory worker finds success making 'capal' sandals
Article:
KANGAR: What began as a setback after losing his factory job during the economic downturn in 2000 turned into a blessing in disguise for Saad Saep, who now earns a living crafting traditional Malay capal sandals.
Saad, 60, from Kampung Chemumar Tengah in Simpang Empat near here, said he never expected the loss of his job at a plastic component manufacturing factory to lead him to a new path as a capal maker.
Recalling how it all began, Saad said he first learned the craft from a friend while living in Sungai Petani, Kedah, before eventually gaining the confidence to produce and sell the traditional footwear independently at night markets and bazaars.
"At that time, I followed a friend and learned how to make capal at his house for about three months. I helped him little by little and noticed there was demand for capal, especially during Ramadan, which sparked my interest in making them," he told Bernama when met recently.
Through perseverance, the sandals he crafted gradually gained popularity among customers, with demand continuing to grow each year, particularly during the festive season.
Saad said he registered his business with the Malaysian Handicraft Development Corporation in 2009 before moving to Perlis a year later to operate his traditional footwear workshop here.
He currently produces eight capal designs using genuine leather and PVC, priced between RM35 and RM250 per pair depending on material quality and design complexity.
"During the Hari Raya Aidilfitri season, my sales can reach about 8,000 pairs due to higher demand for traditional products. Usually, demand increases during Ramadan, with the highest sales typically on the eve of Hari Raya," he said.
Saad, who now employs four workers under the dr.saad brand, said about 90 per cent of the capal produced are sold wholesale to traders in several states.
However, he expressed concern over the future of the heritage footwear as interest among younger generations appears to be fading.
"The older generation understands that capal are a traditional Malay heritage product, but many in the younger generation are not familiar with them. So I will continue making capal to ensure this traditional Malay footwear does not disappear," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's macro position is stronger to weather shock compared to pandemic era
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's stronger ringgit is helping to ease the impact of rising global import prices, although the country's import cover has declined, compared with pre-COVID 19 pandemic levels, said the World Bank's lead economist for Malaysia, Dr Apurva Sanghi.
In a post on his X account (@ApurvaSanghi), he compared the country's economy preparedness to weather the current Middle East war shock against the eve of COVID-19 shocks. Malaysia reported its first COVID-19 positive case on Jan 25, 2020.
Explaining further, Sanghi said Malaysia's macro position is broadly stronger now than during the eve of the pandemic, with most key indicators healthier.
He said Malaysia's gross domestic product (GDP) growth has risen to 5.2 per cent in 2025 from 4.4 per cent in 2019, while real GDP per capita increased to RM48,200 from RM42,500 previously.
"The labour market is also signalling near full employment, with unemployment currently below three per cent, compared with 3.3 per cent before the pandemic," he said.
However, several indicators have weakened relative to the pre-COVID period, including inflation, fiscal balance, debt levels and import cover.
Inflation stood at 1.6 per cent currently versus 0.7 per cent in 2019, while the fiscal deficit has widened slightly to 3.8 per cent of GDP from 3.4 per cent previously, he said, while government debt has also increased to 64.7 per cent of GDP from 52.4 per cent.
Meanwhile, import cover, a key measure of external resilience, has declined to 5.8 months compared with 7.5 months prior to the pandemic.
On inflation, Sanghi said price pressures remain manageable despite stronger growth, reflecting a stable inflation-growth dynamic. However, he noted that uncertainty over inflation expectations could influence the outlook.
On fiscal conditions, he said it is a bit higher now compared to pre-COVID.
"From the pandemic high of six per cent of GDP, the downward trending of the fiscal deficit is encouraging (reflecting subsidy reforms and fiscal consolidation)," he said.
High debt per GDP is a concern, he said, saying that the rising debt interest payments will take up a larger share of government revenue which translates to less room for stimulus if it comes down to that.
Sanghi said Malaysia's economic structure, characterised by a diversified mix of commodities and manufacturing, a flexible exchange rate, and a stable financial system continues to support resilience against external shocks.
Nevertheless, he highlighted several near-term risks, particularly Malaysia's exposure as a highly open, price-taking economy to global supply chain disruptions, especially in fuel, food and fertilisers.
"A strong ringgit offers some respite against higher import prices, although import cover is lower today than on the eve of COVID-19," he said.
He added that persistently high energy prices could affect investment momentum, including in energy-intensive sectors such as artificial intelligence infrastructure.
Sanghi also cautioned that tourism, which was a key growth driver in 2025 and contributed to Malaysia's first services trade surplus in 13 years, may soften due to rising travel costs, particularly jet fuel prices.
On fiscal policy, he said Malaysia's position, while not weak, may require further adjustments, including a review of fuel subsidies.
"To what extent will the government absorb costs through subsidies versus passing them on to consumers remains a key policy consideration," he said, adding that any fuel price adjustments should be implemented gradually to avoid wider economic repercussions.
Overall, Sanghi said Malaysia is entering the current period of uncertainty from a position of relative strength, albeit with narrower fiscal space.
He added that while economic downturns in Malaysia tend to be sharp due to its openness, recoveries are typically swift.
"It's impossible to say how and when the current uncertainty will be resolved.
"Not panic but preparation, and not only by the government but also by the rakyat is what's called for," he added.
On Feb 28, Israel together with the US, launched a military operation against Iran, which was later met with retaliatory strikes by Iran against Israel and several US interests in the Gulf countries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diesel subsidy review: Expert urges targeted, data-driven approach
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As the government considers reviewing the Subsidised Diesel Control Scheme (SKDS) amid concerns over smuggling in Sabah and Sarawak, an expert has called for a shift towards a more targeted and data-driven approach.
Professor Dr Asan Ali Golam Hassan of Universiti Teknologi Malaysia's Azman Hashim International Business School said direct subsidies aimed at specific sectors or groups would be more effective than broad price controls.
"A managed pricing system, backed by appropriate social protection measures, could also help reduce market distortions without causing major disruptions," he told New Straits Times.
He added that digital tools, including real-time tracking and data analytics, were key to improving transparency and ensuring better allocation of resources.
"Moving away from rigid controls towards a system guided by reliable data and proper incentives is the way forward," he said.
Asan Ali noted that Sabah and Sarawak are particularly vulnerable to diesel smuggling due to their geographic and economic conditions.
"Their long coastlines and proximity to Indonesia and the southern Philippines make monitoring cross-border movement far more challenging, especially through informal routes.
"High levels of informal economic activity, limited monitoring infrastructure and significant price differentials create conditions that make smuggling both attractive and difficult to control," he said.
On weaknesses in the current mechanism, he said the issue lay less with policy intent and more with how incentives were structured.
"When diesel is priced significantly lower domestically, it naturally creates room for arbitrage.
"On the ground, mechanisms such as fleet cards and quota allocations remain open to abuse, particularly in the absence of real-time monitoring. The supply chain is also quite layered, making it harder to identify where leakages occur.
"Enforcement capacity, especially across different regions, is not always consistent," he added.
Despite these challenges, Asan Ali said the SKDS had improved the targeting of diesel subsidies compared with the earlier blanket system, particularly for sectors such as logistics, transportation and agriculture.
"That said, it would be difficult to argue that it has fully met its intended objectives. Leakages and smuggling are still very much present. As long as the price gap between Malaysia and neighbouring countries remains wide, the system will continue to be under structural pressure," he said.
On mitigating the impact of any policy changes, he stressed that reforms must be carefully sequenced to avoid unintended consequences, particularly on inflation and the cost of living.
"Targeted support will be critical, especially for lower-income households and sectors such as agriculture, fisheries and public transport.
"A gradual approach will give both businesses and consumers time to adjust. Equally important is clear and consistent communication, so that expectations are well managed and market confidence is maintained," he said.
Recently, government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the
SKDS may undergo a review,
with the Cabinet considering whether to retain the current system or adopt a new approach.
Fahmi, who is also communications minister, said the matter was among key issues raised during the Cabinet meeting, particularly efforts to address diesel smuggling in Sabah and Sarawak.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LLM: Traffic heading to east coast, north congested but under control
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Traffic flow out of the Klang Valley heading to the north and east coast was reported to be congested but remained under control this morning.
A spokesman for the Malaysian Highway Authority (LLM) said on the east coast route, congestion occurred on the Middle Ring Road 2 towards the Gombak Toll Plaza, as well as from Bukit Tinggi and Lentang to the Bentong Petronas station, and from Sungai Dua to the Karak Toll Plaza.
"For the route to the north, congestion occurred on the North-South Expressway from Slim River to Tapah, Gopeng to Simpang Pulai, Ipoh North to the Menora Tunnel, Bandar Baharu to Jawi, Bukit Tambun to Bukit Minyak and Juru to Perai," he said when contacted here today.
He said for the route to the south, congestion occurred from the Port Dickson interchange to Senawang, as well as from Pasir Gudang to Setia Tropika.
"The weather this morning was good, and highway users were advised to comply with speed limits and maintain a safe distance between vehicles throughout the journey," he said.
Road users were also advised to plan their journeys according to the Recommended Travel Time (TTA) schedule issued by the concessionaire and to ensure they had sufficient rest before starting their journey.
The public could obtain the latest traffic information via LLM's toll-free line at 1-800-88-7752 or view closed-circuit television (CCTV) traffic via the agency's official website.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Price tags are for all, says KPDN in Melaka cooking oil raid
Article:
MELAKA: A routine enforcement operation by the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) escalated into a tense exchange when an officer reminded a cashier that price tags must be displayed for all customers, not just Malaysians, during an inspection involving subsidised cooking oil.
The operation, captured in a
video
shared by KPDN Melaka on Facebook, showed officers checking the sale and display of subsidised cooking oil at a retail premises.
During the inspection, the officer confronted workers over the absence and inconsistency of clearly displayed price tags on goods offered to customers.
He said that displaying prices was a basic requirement under existing regulations and applied equally to all customers, regardless of nationality.
The exchange turned tense when the workers attempted to justify the practice, prompting the officer to reiterate that pricing regulations applied across the board and must not discriminate between customers.
In the
video
, the officer was heard issuing a firm warning, stating that price transparency was non-negotiable and that enforcement action could be taken against those who failed to comply.
The incident drew attention online, with many praising the officer's firm approach.
KPDN Melaka said the operation was part of ongoing monitoring efforts to ensure compliance with regulations involving controlled goods, including cooking oil, and to protect consumers from unfair trade practices.
The officer also checked whether the items were sold in accordance with stipulated conditions, including proper labelling, pricing and distribution.
The ministry reminded shop operators to adhere strictly to regulations, including the requirement to display clear and visible price tags on all goods. Failure to do so could result in penalties under relevant consumer protection and price control laws.
KPDN added that enforcement operations would continue nationwide to curb profiteering and ensure that essential goods remained accessible and fairly priced for all.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HSBC flags 20,000 job cuts as AI overhaul unfolds
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: HSBC Holdings Plc is weighing a wave of deep job cuts over the coming years that could ultimately impact around 20,000 roles, or about 10 per cent of its total workforce, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.
Non-client facing roles in global service centers are among those expected to be most impacted as the bank bets on AI, although the assessment is at an early stage, the report said, adding that the review is at an early stage and no final decisions have been made.
HSBC didn't immediately respond to a Reuters' request for a comment.
Accelerating AI adoption is enabling companies to reduce staff in divisions most exposed to automation.
HSBC employed 208,720 full-time equivalent staff at the end of December 2025, according to its annual report.
The potential reductions are a part of a medium-term plan spanning three to five years, and could include not replacing departing staff, as well as cuts tied to business exits or sales, the report said.
The speculation around job cuts come as the London-based bank looks to simplify its operations, cut costs and exit businesses not seen as value-accretive.
In late February, Reuters reported that HSBC had begun a sale process to divest its Singapore life insurance manufacturing business.
Since taking over about 18 months ago, CEO Georges Elhedery has overhauled HSBC by reorganising divisions along East-West lines, exiting sub-scale investment banking units in the US and Europe, and cutting senior management roles.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IGP: Stay calm, avoid inflaming tensions over unauthorised houses of worship
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 19 — Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail today urged Malaysians to remain calm over the issue of unauthorised houses of worship, warning that rising rhetoric risks inflaming racial tensions if left unchecked.
He said what is fundamentally an administrative matter involving land ownership, local authority approvals and compliance with planning laws has increasingly spilled into the public sphere, where it is framed along religious and racial lines.
“Malaysia is a multiracial and multireligious country and all sensitivities must be respected. I urge all parties to reduce negative comments that touch on religion and race,” he told a press conference here, adding that such issues can quickly escalate into wider polemics when debated openly and without restraint on social media.
The police, he said, are closely monitoring recent developments, including online campaigns encouraging public gatherings at places of worship with disputed status, as well as the circulation of provocative videos and statements.
“These are early indicators that could lead to rising racial tensions. It is important for us to act before any situation escalates into a more serious conflict,” he said.
Mohd Khalid stressed that while the police would not hesitate to act firmly against any individual or group attempting to incite racial discord, enforcement alone would not be enough to preserve social harmony.
“It requires cooperation from all parties — community leaders, NGOs and activists — to ensure that sensitive matters are handled responsibly,” he added.
He also welcomed a recent dialogue between Malay and Indian non-governmental organisations aimed at resolving the issue, describing it as a positive step towards maintaining unity and stability.
“The police support and greatly appreciate the commitment and spirit of national unity demonstrated. Efforts like this help ensure racial harmony and national stability,” he said.
On a separate issue, Mohd Khalid addressed claims of double standards in the handling of independent preacher Zamri Vinoth, who was brought to court in handcuffs.
He said such procedures depend on the nature of the offence, noting that some are bailable while others are not.
“The public may view these as different matters, but enforcement is carried out strictly according to the law and its provisions,” he said.

Title: IGP: Activist Tamim may have fled to Saudi Arabia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 19 — The police claimed that activist Tamim Dahri Abdul Razak may have fled to Saudi Arabia and are actively tracking his whereabouts, Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said today.
He said checks indicated Tamim had travelled to Thailand and is now believed to be in Saudi Arabia.
“Our records show he had gone to Thailand, but we have also received information that he may currently be in the Holy Land.
“Action to charge him in court has already been taken, and when the time comes, we will take the necessary steps to bring him in to face the charges,” he told a press conference at Bukit Aman here.
Earlier this week, Mohd Khalid confirmed that four individuals — including Zamri Vinoth and activist Arun Dorasamy — would be charged in connection with alleged religious provocation.
The other two named were Tamim and P. Mahendra Boopathy.
Tamim is set to be charged at the Langkawi Magistrates’ Court under Section 295 of the Penal Code for allegedly damaging a soolam — a sacred symbol for Hindus — at the former site of the Sri Maha Muniswarar temple in Langkawi, Kedah.
The case involving Tamim, 34, was previously postponed after the accused was found to be abroad.
Magistrate Hemy Annerina Haja Mydin has fixed May 17 for mention following an application by the prosecution to defer proceedings.

Title: IGP: Police to record statements from 14 more individuals in alleged plot to topple govt probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 19 — Police may soon call in up to 14 more individuals to assist in investigations into an alleged attempt to topple the government and acts of sabotage against national stability, Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said today.
He said police expect to record statements from all identified individuals as part of ongoing investigations.
“Investigations are still ongoing and the police are examining the matter while carrying out several follow-up actions. It is understood that about 14 more individuals will be called to assist.
“Once all processes are completed, the investigation paper will be finalised and submitted to the Attorney General’s Chambers for further action,” he told a press conference at Bukit Aman here.
When asked if Na’imah Abdul Khalid — the widow of former finance minister Tun Daim Zainuddin — would be among those called, Mohd Khalid declined to comment.
“I do not want to touch on the names of individuals as investigations are still ongoing,” he said.
Na’imah had previously denied allegations contained in a police report linking her to the matter.
Earlier reports said her lawyer Nizamuddin Hamid was among those called in to give statements, alongside another lawyer Amir Zharif Abdullah named in the report.
Nizamuddin has since denied the claims, describing them as a “big lie”.
Police have so far recorded statements from 14 witnesses and are actively tracking down several key individuals believed to be overseas to assist in the probe.

Title: New Sungai Krian Bridge opens, ending long wait for Pusa and Kabong residents, says Uggah
Article:
KUCHING, March 19 — The long wait for residents of Pusa and Kabong to cross Sungai Krian has ended with the official opening of the Sungai Krian Bridge yesterday.
Deputy Premier Datuk Amar Douglas Uggah Embas said the bridge’s completion fulfils a promise to the local community and their elected representatives, allowing it to be used ahead of the upcoming Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebrations.
He highlighted that the bridge is the second of nine expected to be completed this year and a key development in improving rural connectivity in Sarawak.
“This milestone reflects the state government’s commitment under the leadership of Sarawak Premier Datuk Patinggi Tan Sri Abang Johari Tun Openg, not only in planning but also in ensuring that every project benefits the people,” he said during the soft launching ceremony, according to a Sarawak Public Communications Unit (Ukas) report.
Uggah said the Coastal Road project, which links to the Pan Borneo Highway, is a key driver of rural development, and aims to improve access to education, healthcare, and government services, ultimately enhancing residents’ quality of life.
He urged residents to make full use of the new infrastructure to improve living standards and promote inclusive, sustainable community development in line with the Post Covid-19 Development Strategy (PCDS) 2030.
Also present at the soft launch were Betong MP Datuk Dr Richard Rapu, Batang Lupar MP Mohamad Shafizan Kepli, Kalaka assemblyman Mohamad Duri, Beting Maro assemblyman Datuk Razaili Gapor, Krian assemblyman Friday Belik, Senator Pele Peter Tinggom and JKR Sarawak director Datuk Cassidy Morris.
Deputy Premier Datuk Amar Douglas Uggah Embas (5th right) receives a memento from Cassidy after the Sungai Krian Bridge soft launch ceremony March 19, 2026. — Picture via Facebook/JKR Sarawak
Meanwhile, the Public Works Department (JKR) Sarawak said the Sungai Krian Bridge project, part of the Sarawak Coastal Road under Package D4, was executed with a contract value of RM112.5 million.
The 1.227-km bridge features a main structure of 690m with 12 spans, two lanes with an 8m width, and approach roads of 6.5m on both sides, ensuring comfort and safety for users.
“It provides a strategic connection from Batang Saribas Bridge No. 2 to the Roban Interchange on the Pan Borneo Highway.
“Travel time that previously took about 15 minutes using ferry services can now be completed in just a few minutes,” said JKR Sarawak.
The department added that the opening of the Sungai Krian Bridge marks another key achievement in the state’s infrastructure development, making it the tenth bridge to be opened under the Sarawak Coastal Road network initiative. — The Borneo Post

Title: Businessman Victor Chin accuses police of ‘unguided bombardment’ in AMLA probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 19 — Businessman Victor Chin Boon Long today renewed his criticism of the Royal Malaysia Police’s (PDRM) Anti-Money Laundering (AMLA) unit, accusing it of “persecuting innocent people” and heavy-handedness in its investigation against him.
In a press statement today, Chin said he chose to break his six-month silence as his situation had devolved to “extremely unhealty” levels.
He described the police unit’s actions as an “intensive aerial bombardment exercise” using “unguided bombs” to freeze any bank account remotely associated with him, his friends, or his business partners.
“Anyone who knows me becomes collateral damage,” Chin claimed.
“We are living in an environment where anyone who knows Victor Chin or calls Victor Chin their boss, gets AMLA-ed. Their accounts will be frozen.”
He alleged that to date, more than 500 accounts have been frozen without proper due diligence.
According to Chin, many of these belong to individuals and businesses whose only “crime” was holding, trading, or transacting in shares of MMAG Holdings Bhd, NexG Bhd, and NexG Bina Bhd, companies linked to him.
NexG is a leading Malaysian security technology firm, formerly Datasonic Group Berhad, specialising in secure identification solutions like MyKad national ID cards, passport chips, biometrics, and smart card systems for government and commercial us
Chin directly blamed these actions for the severe crash in the share prices of those companies in mid-October 2025, which coincided with the start of the AMLA investigation.
While stating he understood the freezing of his personal accounts, he argued that the indiscriminate freezing of others’ has caused massive repercussions, effectively “blacklisting them for life” from financial institutions globally.
The businessman insisted he has done nothing wrong and has fully cooperated with the authorities, highlighting that he returned to Malaysia on December 29, 2025, at PDRM’s request, provided a full statement, and was remanded for three days before being released.
In his statement, Chin also made a serious allegation of high-level interference, claiming there were “magic hands” facilitating the unauthorised unfreezing and re-freezing of accounts belonging to Rembulan Lestari Sdn Bhd and Skylimit Alliance Sdn Bhd to enable a share transfer involving NexG.
He urged Bursa Malaysia and the Securities Commission to investigate the matter.
He ended with a direct appeal to Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail, calling for his intervention to ensure a fair investigation free from “third-party interferences,” describing the current situation as a matter of national security that could damage investor confidence in Malaysia.
Chin also appended a document in which he apologised to various public officials, corporate figures, and companies over his investigation.
On Monday, the IGP confirmed that “inspections” were being carried out at several premises linked to the businessman.
The investigation gained momentum following instructions from the Cabinet for a multi-agency sweep into claims of corporate manipulation.
The issue first made international headlines on February 11 via a Bloomberg report, which alleged that a group of businessmen collaborated with MACC officials, including Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki, to pressure corporate figures during company takeovers.
Azam Baki has vehemently denied these claims and is currently suing Bloomberg for RM100 million in damages.

Title: Culvert installation underway to tackle flooding at Jalan Hua Lai, Jalan Hock Ming in Bawang Assan, says SUPP
Article:
SIBU, March 19 — Sarawak United Peoples’ Party (SUPP) Bawang Assan branch has initiated an infrastructure improvement project along Jalan Hua Lai and Jalan Hock Ming, involving the installation of culverts.
The branch’s chairman Cr Kevin Lau said the project aims to enhance the local drainage system and address long-standing flooding issues faced by residents in the area.
“Upgrading the infrastructure in the area is crucial in improving the living environment and quality of life for residents and affirmed that similar community issues will continue to be addressed to ensure the well-being of the people,” he said in a statement.
The affected roads have long suffered from poor drainage, resulting in frequent flooding during heavy rainfall.
This has not only disrupted traffic but also inconvenienced nearby residents in their daily lives.
With active assistance and feedback from local community leader Kapitan Tang Chuo Ging, the issue was brought to attention and subsequently included in the improvement plan.
The project is being implemented by the Sibu Rural District Council (SRDC), with culvert installation works started yesterday.
Upon completion, the project is expected to significantly improve wastewater discharge and overall drainage efficiency, thereby reducing the occurrence of flooding in the area.
Lau also expressed his appreciation, commending the swift action taken by SRDC and thanking Tang for his efforts in highlighting and coordinating the community’s concerns. — The Borneo Post

Title: ETS tickets 86pc already sold as KTMB boosts trips for Raya travel
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, March 19 — A total of 217,000 Electric Train Service (ETS) tickets for the Hari Raya Aidilfitri travel period have been sold as of 9 am today, out of 257,000 tickets offered.
Keretapi Tanah Melayu Berhad (KTMB) Chief Operating Officer Afzar Zakariya said the figure represents about 86 per cent of total ticket sales, with around 35,000 tickets still available, mainly for trips into Kuala Lumpur.
“The most in-demand tickets are for routes departing from the capital, especially from Kuala Lumpur to Ipoh and Padang Besar. The northern sector is fully sold out, while limited seats remain for the southern routes.
“It can be said that tickets for balik kampung journeys are almost gone, while tickets for trips into Kuala Lumpur are still available,” he said after inspecting operations at JB Sentral today.
To meet the surge in demand during the festive season, Afzar said ETS services have been increased from the usual 46 trips per day to up to 70 trips daily.
Currently, 62 daily trips have been opened for booking, with the remaining eight to be released depending on demand or if existing tickets are fully taken up.
“If all 70 daily trips are activated, total ticket capacity could rise to 285,000,” he said.
He advised the public to plan their journeys early and purchase return tickets in advance, especially for trips back to Kuala Lumpur after Aidilfitri, as demand is expected to spike when people return to work. — Bernama

Title: Natural Resources Ministry pledges support for family of border survey officer swept away in Sarawak river, says Arthur Kurup
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, March 19 — The Ministry of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability (NRES) has assured that the welfare of the family of a Department of Survey and Mapping Malaysia (JUPEM) officer who went missing in Sungai Pandaruan, Limbang, will remain a priority throughout the ongoing search and rescue operation.
Minister, Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup, said he has instructed the department to extend all necessary assistance, including moral support and welfare coordination, to the victim’s family.
“Let us all pray that the search and rescue team’s efforts are eased and that the victim’s family is granted strength and resilience in facing this ordeal.
“NRES will continue to closely monitor the operation and provide full cooperation to the authorities in Sarawak to ensure every effort is made to locate the victim,” he said in a statement today.
Arthur said he had been informed of the tragic incident involving a JUPEM officer who was reportedly swept away by strong currents while carrying out boundary surveying work along the Malaysia–Brunei border at Sungai Pandaruan.
“We understand that this is an extremely difficult and trying time for them. The victim is a dedicated civil servant.
“Boundary surveying is no easy task. It demands great courage and sacrifice in safeguarding the nation’s sovereignty and interests, often in challenging terrain,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Fire razes 10 homes in Lutong, Sarawak; 42 homeless as firefighters battle dense layout (VIDEO)
Article:
MIRI, March 19 — A devastating fire at the Kampung Pasir squatter colony in Lutong here early this morning has left 42 villagers homeless.
The Sarawak Fire and Rescue Department (Bomba) said it received an emergency call at 4.06am and dispatched firefighters from the Lutong fire station, assisted by teams from Lopeng and Miri Central.
Upon arrival at 4.15am, firefighters found the blaze had already engulfed several non-permanent houses in the densely populated settlement at Lorong 4, a stone’s throw away from the Lutong fire station and about 7km from the other two stations.
